interactions.summary: Jiang describes reinventing himself by dying to the Yale ego through cooking school, jiu-jitsu, stand-up, climbing, skydiving, and singing; humiliation becomes education in humility, empathy, resilience, ego, fear, and love.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Interview exchange: SPEAKER_00 is host Jack Neel or ad read; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang unless noted.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization is consistent enough for this packet: SPEAKER_00 carries host questions, intro, outro, or sponsor reads; SPEAKER_01 carries Jiang answers. Sponsor ad segments are not Jiang claims.

claims.claim: Jiang says he redesigned his Yale education by entering ordinary humiliating practices where elite credentials meant nothing, using failure to dismantle ego.
Jiang defines real education as risk-taking, trying new things, self-reflection, humility, empathy, and resilience, not elite schools and grades.

signature_moments.moment: The chef refusing to touch his dishes punctures Yale superiority.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I'm not eating this crap. Go away, man.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the comic humiliation that starts the education reversal.

signature_moments.moment: Ego and fear become the two greatest enemies.
signature_moments.source_phrase: your two greatest enemies are ego and fear
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This distills the whole autobiographical transformation.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's phrase for replacing elite instruction with ordinary practices that force humility.
The two inner enemies Jiang says block love and openness.

chronology_notes.note: This packet narrates post-Yale reinvention before marriage and family, not a geopolitical prediction.
